title: Modi-Carney G7 Bilateral Resets India-Canada Energy and Trade Partnership
lede: Prime Ministers Narendra Modi and Mark Carney met at the G7 Summit in Evian on June 16, 2026, to advance a forward-looking strategic partnership centred on LNG, coal, and resilient supply chains.

sections.context: The bilateral meeting between Modi and Carney marks the continuation of momentum built since the Canadian Prime Minister's March 2026 visit to India, the first by a Canadian PM in eight years. The two leaders reaffirmed commitment to a forward-looking strategic partnership, explicitly acknowledging complementarities between Indian and Canadian economies. The timing at the G7 Summit in France signals that India-Canada cooperation is now a multilateral concern, with both leaders emphasizing the role of resilient and reliable supply chains in strengthening global energy and food security—positioning their bilateral ties within broader geopolitical and economic resilience frameworks. The leaders reviewed progress in commercial arrangements relating to LNG, LPG, and metallurgical coal, indicating that energy security cooperation has moved from declarative statements into concrete commercial negotiations.
sections.threads: The Modi-Carney meeting sits within a broader recalibration of India-Canada relations following years of diplomatic friction. The successful reset under Carney's leadership, including his [[india-ca-pm-carneys-india-visit-marks-canadas-diplomatic-reset-a-2026|first visit to India in March 2026]], has created political space for substantive economic engagement. The focus on LNG, LPG, and coal reflects India's energy security priorities and Canada's position as a reliable resource supplier. Additionally, the mention of ongoing high-level ministerial exchanges—including the Indian Minister of Commerce and Industry's recent visit to Canada and a forthcoming Canadian trade mission—points to the simultaneous negotiation of the India-Canada Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement (CEPA), which has been establishing a [[india-ca-india-canada-cepa-framework-finalized-50-billion-trade-2026|$50 billion trade target]]. The G7 setting also underscores how bilateral energy and trade cooperation is being framed as part of a shared democratic alliance strategy.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor the outcomes of the forthcoming Canadian trade mission to India and the pace of CEPA negotiations through the remainder of 2026. The explicit focus on LNG and metallurgical coal suggests that energy infrastructure deals and long-term supply contracts could be finalized within the next 6-12 months. Key decision points will include whether the two countries formalize commercial arrangements for Canadian LNG exports to India and the signing of the CEPA framework. The sustainability of this reset will depend on whether both governments can manage domestic political pressures—particularly around immigration and security concerns that have historically complicated the relationship. Watch for whether high-level visits continue at the current frequency and whether sectoral working groups on energy, trade, and investment produce tangible outcomes. The G7 context also raises questions about whether India-Canada cooperation will be explicitly coordinated with broader Western supply-chain diversification strategies.
